summary.leadingMessage: AI supplier diversification is not keeping pace with concentration in the highest-value lanes.

items.boardQuestion: Where is one model provider shaping too much of the product and delivery roadmap?
items.currentPosture: The product surface is shipping well, but the estate still leans too heavily on one core model provider.
items.concentrationNarrative: One vendor is absorbing too much of the model-layer decision surface.
items.operatingReality: Prompt behavior, eval baselines, and customer-facing product expectations still revolve around one foundation model contract.
items.riskHeadline: Model strategy is moving faster than vendor diversification.
items.concentrationSignal: Critical routes still assume one provider's latency, tool semantics, and release cadence.
items.blockingIssue: The fallback model path is not strong enough to absorb a commercial or reliability shock.
items.headline: One model vendor is carrying too much of the AI control plane.
items.narrative: The upside is real, but the board story is still too dependent on one provider staying cheap, stable, and policy-friendly.
items.nextMove: Stand up one board-visible fallback path for every customer-facing model workflow above the premium margin threshold.

concentrationAssessment.message: Vendor concentration is high enough to distort the board and investor dependency story.

switchingAssessment.message: Switching cost is high enough to weaken negotiating leverage materially.

contractAssessment.message: Contract exposure is high enough to give one supplier too much pricing power.

fallbackAssessment.message: Fallback readiness is uneven and should be strengthened.

complianceAssessment.message: Compliance dependency is visible and should be made more portable.

couplingAssessment.message: Platform coupling is high enough to make supplier exit or repricing materially harder.

items.lane: Cloud AI bundle and hosting estate
items.boardQuestion: Which vendor bundle is becoming sticky because compute, identity, and AI workloads are being bought together?
items.currentPosture: The bundle is convenient, though the hosting and AI layers are getting harder to price or move independently.
items.concentrationNarrative: Convenience is drifting into stack-level dependency.
items.operatingReality: Inference, auth, storage, and telemetry are still packaged closely enough that exit math looks heavier every quarter.
items.riskHeadline: Platform leverage is improving while switching leverage is getting worse.
items.concentrationSignal: The same provider keeps winning adjacent layers because procurement and technical handoffs reward the bundle.
items.blockingIssue: The team lacks one clean isolation boundary for moving AI workloads without pulling infra and identity along with them.
opportunityMoves: separate identity from inference renewal windows
items.headline: The cloud AI bundle is getting too sticky.
items.narrative: The board should see this as a bundling problem, not just a vendor preference problem.
items.nextMove: Force one renewal packet that prices AI, cloud, and identity together and separately before the next contract cycle.

concentrationAssessment.message: Vendor concentration is high enough to distort the board and investor dependency story.

switchingAssessment.message: Switching cost is high enough to weaken negotiating leverage materially.

contractAssessment.message: Contract exposure is high enough to give one supplier too much pricing power.

fallbackAssessment.message: Fallback readiness is uneven and should be strengthened.

complianceAssessment.message: Compliance dependency is visible and should be made more portable.

couplingAssessment.message: Platform coupling is high enough to make supplier exit or repricing materially harder.

items.boardQuestion: Which vendor relationship is creating hidden compliance dependency because policy, logging, and audit workflows depend on one trust stack?
items.currentPosture: The trust stack is legible, though too many compliance narratives still depend on one outside vendor's artifact model.
items.concentrationNarrative: Compliance packaging is reusable, but still too dependent on one external proof shape.
items.operatingReality: Buyers can read the trust packet, though the underlying evidence model still assumes one vendor's semantics too often.
items.riskHeadline: Proof portability is weaker than the public trust story suggests.
items.concentrationSignal: Policy exports, audit evidence, and disclosure packets still map best to one provider's control framework.
items.blockingIssue: The estate lacks one portable control vocabulary that survives outside the current trust vendor contract.
items.headline: Compliance proof is still too dependent on one vendor's shape.
items.narrative: The trust story is credible, but the evidence model still needs more portability before it looks robust under diligence.
items.nextMove: Publish one vendor-neutral evidence index and force every trust packet to resolve through it first.

concentrationAssessment.message: Vendor concentration is visible and should be diversified before the next review cycle.

switchingAssessment.message: Switching cost is rising and should be reduced with cleaner fallback paths.

contractAssessment.message: Contract exposure is visible and should be negotiated harder.

fallbackAssessment.message: Fallback readiness is strong enough in this lane.

complianceAssessment.message: Compliance dependency is high enough to trap evidence and audit posture in one vendor stack.

couplingAssessment.message: Platform coupling is rising and should be ring-fenced.

items.lane: Data pipelines and model fine-tuning estate
items.boardQuestion: Where is vendor concentration getting worse because training data, prompts, or evaluations are hard to move out cleanly?
items.currentPosture: The data layer is productive, though egress and reuse constraints still make alternative-vendor moves feel expensive.
items.concentrationNarrative: The estate can grow, but it cannot pivot cheaply enough yet.
items.operatingReality: Embeddings, eval traces, prompt assets, and fine-tuning assumptions still sit too close to one provider's stack.
items.riskHeadline: Portability remains weaker than procurement leverage.
items.concentrationSignal: The migration packet still requires too much format cleanup and too much model-specific adaptation work.
items.blockingIssue: There is no one durable export contract for prompts, evals, and supporting data flows.
opportunityMoves: dry-run one migration path every quarter
items.headline: Data portability is still too weak for comfortable vendor leverage.
items.narrative: The board should treat data egress and eval portability as a real supplier-risk control, not just an engineering preference.
items.nextMove: Create one provider-neutral export bundle for prompts, evals, and tuning traces before the next major vendor negotiation.

concentrationAssessment.message: Vendor concentration is visible and should be diversified before the next review cycle.

switchingAssessment.message: Switching cost is high enough to weaken negotiating leverage materially.

contractAssessment.message: Contract exposure is visible and should be negotiated harder.

fallbackAssessment.message: Fallback readiness is uneven and should be strengthened.

complianceAssessment.message: Compliance dependency is visible and should be made more portable.

couplingAssessment.message: Platform coupling is high enough to make supplier exit or repricing materially harder.

items.boardQuestion: Which AI supplier is gaining pricing power because revenue workflows are too dependent on one experience layer?
items.currentPosture: The growth surface is compelling, though margin sensitivity is still too exposed to one AI vendor's pricing moves.
items.concentrationNarrative: Commercial upside is real, but pricing leverage is uneven.
items.operatingReality: Revenue workflows now assume one supplier's output quality in ways that reduce negotiation flexibility.
items.riskHeadline: Margin quality is improving while supplier leverage is slipping.
items.concentrationSignal: Growth workflows keep adding higher-value use cases faster than multi-vendor negotiating leverage is improving.
items.blockingIssue: The commercial team still lacks one clear downgrade or alternate-provider story for key AI-enhanced experiences.
opportunityMoves: tie usage growth to pricing tiers
negotiate with a visible fallback story
items.headline: Revenue AI is creating pricing-power exposure.
items.narrative: This lane needs a stronger negotiating posture before the next AI usage step-up hardens supplier leverage.
items.nextMove: Attach one alternate-provider or degraded-mode plan to every revenue-critical AI workflow above the margin review threshold.

concentrationAssessment.message: Vendor concentration is visible and should be diversified before the next review cycle.

switchingAssessment.message: Switching cost is rising and should be reduced with cleaner fallback paths.

contractAssessment.message: Contract exposure is high enough to give one supplier too much pricing power.

fallbackAssessment.message: Fallback readiness is uneven and should be strengthened.

complianceAssessment.message: Compliance dependency is contained enough in this lane.

couplingAssessment.message: Platform coupling is rising and should be ring-fenced.

items.boardQuestion: Which leadership-facing lane still looks diversified on paper but lacks enough real multi-vendor readiness to prove it?
items.currentPosture: The narrative is broad, though the actual provider fallback and substitution drill cadence is still too thin.
items.concentrationNarrative: The strategy sounds diversified faster than the implementation proves it.
items.operatingReality: The portfolio references many vendors, but too few surfaces show hardened alternate-path readiness.
items.riskHeadline: Narrative diversification is ahead of operational diversification.
items.concentrationSignal: Too many leadership surfaces can describe supplier options but cannot yet demonstrate clean substitution under pressure.
items.blockingIssue: There is no one recurring drill packet that proves a concentrated lane can survive provider disruption or repricing.
items.headline: The diversification story still needs more proof.
items.narrative: This lane should make fallback readiness explicit before leaders lean too hard on the breadth of the vendor narrative alone.
items.nextMove: Add one quarterly substitution drill and one confidence score to every concentrated executive-intelligence lane.

Data pipelines and model fine-tuning estate

verification: Synthetic AI vendor concentration data only - no live invoices, procurement records, legal contracts, or private vendor performance logs are included.
Scores are modeled to show how Kinetic Gain can expose supplier concentration, switching friction, pricing leverage, fallback weakness, and compliance dependency in one board-readable surface.
All routes are read-only and demonstrate executive supplier-risk diagnosis, not procurement advice, legal guidance, or live vendor-selection instructions.
